Now, once you have your four quadrants figured out and you're comfortable and content, it's time for the letter of intent as I bust a rhyme. You've done it now. You feel comfortable. You've found and analyzed a deal that you believe is amazing. You're ready to move forward. That leads to the next big question. How do I get a seller to agree, agree in writing to sell me their deal? The first step now is to write a letter of intent. This is a written document. Should be one page showing your intent to purchase their property. In this simple letter, no lawyer is required, plan to sell yourself with supporting amendments and or letters to this one page LOI. Make the terms of your deal clear and hard, touching on five major points. Number one, the price you're willing to pay. Number two, the closing date. Number three, financing you'll be using. Number four, the period or time of your due diligence. And number five, your security deposit. By signing and sending an LOI, you're giving your intent to purchase and hoping your offer isn't met with stiff competition. But if it is, make sure your letter of intent is supported by other documentation that supports your price, your down payment, your NOI even in your four quadrants and the cash on cash. So I might even provide a supporting document and amendment to my LOI. A letter that says, hey look, I know your property is performing at this. We think we can lift that. I'm willing to pay this price. If you're met with stiff competition, it's your LOI that should cut through the clutter and bring you to the top of the list or considerations of the seller. No matter the scenario, it always makes you look more professional when you provide a simple to the point LOI that provides the key points. Another perk to LOIs, when I pull together a purchase and sale agreement, I usually wind up spending $25,000 in legal fees minimally, granted on a small four or eight unit deal, you can't even afford legal. It won't produce enough income to support the legal. You'll have to wait to recoup that in the years to come if you ever sell the property. Here you go right to contract with a PSA. Even then an attorney should review everything which costs money and you don't need to do that with a simple letter of intent. Remember, every LOI will include five simple elements. Price, closing date, financing, due diligence. It's typical in most markets that you would ask for a 30 day due diligence, but the shorter the time the better. We like to ask for 21 or less. And number five, your deposit, security deposit. Also you need to add when that security deposit would go hard, non-refundable in the future. There are also a few other things that you should include on the backside of your LOI, like your bio, how many properties you've owned or done, who you financed with if you had preferred financing with someone. A letter saying that you're preferred borrow and anything else that might be positive about yourself. If you have cash in the bank to put down on this deal, show them a statement where you're liquid to do so. The question to ask yourself is your LOI, sell yourself effectively and efficiently so your offer is irresistible. And remember when writing your LOI, go back to that earlier lesson, commit first and figure it out later. Show up. Let your LOI express that you're showing up, that you want the deal, that you're committed to buying the deal. Do not get into the complexities that you haven't figured out yet, like financing or your due diligence or the title search or you got to get a lawyer or the PSA. Commit first, figure the rest out later. Yes, this approach can get you into trouble. Of course it can. But it's still the right approach to get the seller's attention and pick you as a buyer. Without this commitment, you'll never get close to a deal. You'll spend all your time talking, thinking, backing up, Excel spreadsheeting, culling intel but never closing a deal. Hell, you won't even get a deal under contract. Your LOI forces your hand a bit, but it also forces the seller's hand a bit with that letter and now you need to find the money to do the deal if you don't have it already. Multiple times I've had the first and even the second lender pass on my deal even though I had an LOI. It's possible had I gotten rejection from two lenders, I would have just walked away. But because I was committed with an LOI, with a clock ticking, with me spending time and energy and resources on due diligence, I continue to look for banks that would finance my first deal. I've gotten to the third lender. The third lender couldn't wait to give me the money and that's the deal I made millions on. That was my first score. It happened because I had a letter of intent that the seller signed that gave us permission to move forward with a deal. At that point, I could adjust the terms of price if needed or down payment with the bank. Look maybe you've got to tweak a few details to appease the bank or the lender or maybe you have to tweak a few details to appease yourself and your investors. Maybe, maybe the seller gets cold feet and you have to pay a little extra to close the deal. In my case, I got the financing lockdown even though the first two lenders said no. Within the terms of the LOI, we committed, we got a PSA, purchased a sale agreement, we got a lawyer involved, found out the bank wanted an LOC, that's when I hired a lawyer to do such and we closed the deal. Without a commitment from you and the seller, you're not going to get the money to move forward with your deals. Now the main takeaway here is what? You need to understand the power of the LOI. At the end of the day, the deal is everything. No deals, no profits. Once you find a deal, commit to the deal. Commit to the seller, write the LOI. Don't worry about the details. You'll find the money. You'll find the financing. You'll work out the legal without a commitment. You're not going to find money, financing, and you're not going to close the deal. With a commitment, everything is possible. Otherwise, expect to quit before you even get started. Use our simple LOI. Just know this, in every market, you might be required to tweak your LOI to the conditions and the culture of your market.
